subtopics.title: Asexual Reproduction
common_misconceptions: Asexual reproduction only happens in simple organisms
Offspring from asexual reproduction are genetically identical (mostly true but mutations occur)
subtopics.teaching_note: Asexual: one parent, no gametes, offspring genetically identical (clones). Binary fission: Amoeba, bacteria. Budding: yeast, Hydra. Fragmentation: Spirogyra, planaria. Vegetative propagation: natural (runners in grass, rhizomes in ginger, tubers in yam, bulbs in onion) and artificial (cuttings, grafting, layering, tissue culture). Advantages: fast, no need for pollinators, preserves desirable traits. Disadvantages: no genetic variation, diseases affect all.

subtopics.title: Sexual Reproduction in Plants and Animals
common_misconceptions: Fertilization always occurs in the uterus (occurs in fallopian tube)
Women produce new eggs throughout life (all eggs present at birth)
Pollination is the same as fertilization
subtopics.teaching_note: Sexual: two parents, gametes fuse, genetic variation. Male system: testes (sperm + testosterone), epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicle, prostate, urethra, penis. Female system: ovaries (eggs + estrogen/progesterone), fallopian tube, uterus, cervix, vagina. Menstrual cycle: ~28 days. Follicular phase (days 1-13), ovulation (day 14), luteal phase (days 15-28). Spermatogenesis continuous from puberty. Oogenesis: all primary oocytes present at birth. Fertilization: sperm + egg → zygote in fallopian tube → implants in uterus.

subtopics.title: Mendelian Genetics
common_misconceptions: Dominant allele is always more common in population
Recessive traits disappear in heterozygotes (they are just not expressed)
Phenotype ratio is always 3:1 (only for complete dominance monohybrid)
subtopics.teaching_note: Mendel's Laws: 1) Law of Segregation - alleles separate during gamete formation. 2) Law of Independent Assortment - genes for different traits assort independently. Monohybrid cross (one trait): TT × tt → all Tt (F1), Tt × Tt → 3:1 (F2). Dihybrid cross (two traits): 9:3:3:1 ratio. Test cross: unknown genotype × homozygous recessive to determine genotype. Incomplete dominance: pink flowers from red × white (1:2:1). Co-dominance: both alleles expressed (AB blood group). Sex-linked inheritance: genes on X chromosome (colour blindness, haemophilia). Blood groups: IA, IB, IO alleles → A, B, AB, O phenotypes.

subtopics.title: Ecosystem and Ecology
common_misconceptions: Producers only include green plants (also algae, some bacteria)
Decomposers are not important (they recycle nutrients)
Food chains have no maximum length (usually 4-5 levels due to energy loss)
subtopics.teaching_note: Ecology: study of organisms and their environment. Abiotic: light, temperature, water, soil, pH, humidity. Biotic: living components. Trophic levels: producers (autotrophs, level 1), primary consumers (herbivores, level 2), secondary consumers (carnivores, level 3), tertiary consumers (level 4), decomposers. Energy flow: unidirectional, 10% energy transfer between levels (Lindeman's 10% law). Pyramid of numbers, biomass, energy. Nigerian ecosystems: tropical rainforest (Cross River), savanna (Guinea, Sudan, Sahel), freshwater (Niger Delta), mangrove swamp.
